summaries.Hongseok:
Category purpose  
“Hongseok” houses all messages sent by (or explicitly on behalf of) the colleague/stakeholder named Hongseok. Think executive-style traffic: status check-ins, project guidance, approvals, quick questions, meeting invites, and occasional FYIs or resource links.

Typical sender intent  
Hongseok usually wants one of three things: (1) a fast update on current progress or blockers, (2) a decision/approval turn-around, or (3) scheduling coordination. Tone is direct and concise; messages rarely exceed one screen.

Handling & response strategy  
1. Flag as High priority; aim to acknowledge within 2 business hours.  
2. Skim for any explicit deadline, decision request, or open question; surface these in your reply’s first sentence.  
3. When providing updates, use a mini-bullet list (≤5 bullets) with clear owner, status, and next step.  
4. If information is pending, confirm receipt and give an ETA.  
5. File completed threads to “archive > Hongseok” to keep the category uncluttered.

Key preferences  
• Prefers action-oriented subject lines (“Update – Feature X, EOD Wed”).  
• Avoid large attachments; share drive links.  
• Keep greetings formal (“Hi Hongseok,”) and sign-offs short (“Best, [Name]”).
summaries.Akify:
Akify category overview  
This folder captures any message that references the Akify product, brand, or team. Typical themes include: (1) customer support—setup questions, troubleshooting, feature requests, bug reports; (2) account or billing issues—subscription upgrades, invoices, payment failures, refunds; (3) sales and partnerships—demo requests, reseller inquiries, API/integration proposals; (4) internal or legal notices—maintenance windows, security advisories, compliance or privacy documentation. Senders usually seek quick resolution, technical guidance, or decision-maker contact.

Triage/handling:  
• Prioritise production-blocking issues and paid-plan customers first; acknowledge within 1 business hour, resolve or escalate within 24.  
• Route billing matters to Finance, legal/compliance to the General Counsel, and feature feedback to Product.  
• Tag tickets by type (Support, Billing, Sales, Legal) and severity (P0–P3).  
• Maintain a clear audit trail in the CRM and link to any internal Jira/Asana tasks.

Response style: empathetic, concise, solution-oriented; include relevant knowledge-base links, ETA, and next steps. Adhere to NDA and data-privacy policies; never disclose internal roadmaps or other customers’ data.
summaries.DAPLab:
DAPLab emails generally center on the day-to-day running of the research lab, data-driven project work, and collaboration logistics. Typical messages include:  

• Internal updates on project milestones, experiment results, and data analysis findings.  
• Requests for meeting times, lab space or equipment reservations, and access to datasets or software licenses.  
• Inquiries from students or external partners about joining studies, sharing code, or co-authoring papers.  
• Administrative notices on funding opportunities, compliance training, and grant-report deadlines.  

Sender intent is usually to coordinate resources, seek technical feedback, or obtain timely approvals that keep projects moving.  

Triage guidance:  
1. Acknowledge all operational or resource requests within one business day and confirm next steps.  
2. Forward funding or compliance items to the PI and grants administrator immediately.  
3. Log any data-sharing or participant-related requests for audit purposes before approving.  

Response style: concise, action-oriented, and CC relevant team leads. Follow lab data-security protocols when attaching files, and use the lab’s shared calendar for scheduling to avoid conflicts.
summaries.DSL:
DSL  
Scope: Messages about Digital Subscriber Line internet service—installation, activation, performance, outages, equipment, plan changes, or billing questions tied to a DSL account.

Sender intent & common asks  
• Start/transfer service, check availability, or request a site survey  
• Report slow speeds, intermittent sync, or total loss of connectivity  
• Replace/return modems, request firmware files, or reset credentials  
• Upgrade/downgrade speed tier, suspend, or cancel service  
• Seek credits for downtime or clarification of DSL-specific charges

Triage & handling  
1. Verify the customer’s DSL phone number/account, service address, and current plan.  
2. Run remote line/port tests; if inconclusive, book a field tech within SLA (standard: 24 h urban, 48 h rural).  
3. For performance complaints, attach latest speed/log screenshots to the ticket and share self-help steps (reboot, filter checks, isolation test).  
4. Coordinate with Billing for adjustment requests once a fault ticket is closed.  
5. Escalate chronic loop issues to Network Engineering with at least two trouble-ticket IDs.

Style & policy  
Respond within 2 business hours, use clear step-by-step instructions, and never expose internal DSLAM IPs or proprietary diagnostics.
summaries.DESDR:
DESDR emails center on “Design & Engineering Service/Document Requests.” Typical messages include new DESDR form submissions, follow-up questions on earlier requests, comments from reviewers, or notifications that supporting drawings/specs have been updated. Senders—usually project engineers, drafters, or PMs—want one of three things: (1) approval or rejection of a proposed design change, (2) clarification of technical requirements, or (3) confirmation that all documentation is complete and logged in the DESDR tracker.

Triage: log each email against its DESDR reference number, verify that required attachments (rev-controlled drawings, calculations, risk assessments) are present, then route to the assigned discipline lead. Acknowledge receipt within one business day; provide a target decision date or next review milestone. Escalate urgent safety-related changes immediately.

Response style: concise, bullet-pointed actions, include the DESDR number in the subject line, and CC the project QA/QC distribution list. Follow document-control policy—no uncontrolled file formats, preserve revision history, and archive final approvals in the central repository.
summaries.Karthik:
Karthik: All correspondence sent by, cc’ing, or explicitly about the contact “Karthik.” Messages usually contain project updates, task assignments, technical clarifications, meeting invites, or feedback on deliverables. The sender’s intent is typically to move a project forward—requesting status, sharing decisions, supplying reference material, or asking for quick approval.  

Triage & handling:  
• Scan for action items first; Karthik’s notes often include clear asks or deadlines.  
• Acknowledge receipt the same business day, even if a full answer will follow later.  
• Capture tasks in your tracker, then reply with a concise summary of next steps, owner, and ETA.  
• If the email includes attachments or code snippets, save them to the relevant project workspace before replying.  
• For meeting requests, propose slots or confirm attendance within 4 hours.  

Style preferences: Keep replies structured (greeting, 1-sentence context, numbered responses), avoid jargon, and highlight decisions in bold or a bullet list. Escalate blockers immediately; silence can delay the entire project.
summaries.CapOne:
CapOne  

What belongs here  
• Any email originating from Capital One (credit cards, checking, auto loans, or savings).  
• Statement-ready notifications, payment-due reminders, transaction alerts, fraud/security messages, rate or policy updates, promotional offers, and customer-service case replies.  

Sender intent & common requests  
• Notify me of an upcoming payment, completed payment, or new statement.  
• Alert me to suspicious activity and request verification.  
• Confirm profile, card-replacement, or dispute actions.  
• Market new products, card benefits, or rate changes.  

Handling / triage strategy  
1. Security-related or fraud alerts → same-day review; log in to the Capital One portal to confirm activity; respond or call using the number on the back of the card—never via embedded links.  
2. Payment/statement notices → file under “Billing → CapOne Statements,” schedule payment if needed, then mark done.  
3. Service-case replies → update ticket in CRM; reply within 1 business day.  
4. Pure marketing → tag “CapOne-Promo” and defer unless immediately relevant.  

Policies & style  
• Never transmit full card numbers or personal data in replies.  
• Use the secure message center for account-specific responses.
summaries.ugrad research:
uGrad Research category  

What lands here  
• Messages from undergraduates or academic staff about research opportunities, course‐credit projects, or summer fellowships.  
• Requests to join my lab/group, inquiries about open RA positions, or follow-ups on previously submitted interest forms.  
• Updates on ongoing undergraduate projects: progress reports, data-sharing links, draft manuscripts, IRB or safety paperwork, and meeting-time polls.  

Typical sender intent  
Students seek approval to start or continue a project, clarification of expectations (hours, credit, stipend), or feedback on deliverables. Faculty or coordinators may circulate application deadlines, funding decisions, or compliance reminders.  

Triage & response  
1. Same-day skim for hard deadlines or compliance items; flag “urgent” if forms are due within 72 h.  
2. If space is available, send template “next-steps” email (eligibility checklist, résumé request, possible interview slots). Otherwise, politely decline and add to wait-list sheet.  
3. For active mentees, log progress in tracking spreadsheet and schedule feedback within 2 business days. Forward safety/IRB docs to compliance officer.  

Key policies  
Observe FERPA and lab data-confidentiality rules; no student grades or IDs in plain text. Encourage concise, professional communication; replies within 48 h weekdays.
summaries.Rec Letter:
Rec Letter  

What lands here  
Messages asking me to write or submit a reference or recommendation letter, provide a referee form, or confirm I served as a recommender. This also includes deadline reminders, portal login instructions, and thank-you follow-ups from applicants, graduate schools, scholarship committees, or employers.

Sender intent  
• Secure my agreement to recommend them  
• Supply required materials (CV, draft statement, form links)  
• Clarify content, word limits, or submission process  
• Nudge about approaching deadlines or status

Triage & response  
1. Acknowledge within 24 hrs; confirm whether I can meet the deadline.  
2. If accepting, request résumé, target program description, due date, and any required forms.  
3. Calendar the due date and set a 1-week advance reminder.  
4. On completion, send confirmation to requester and retain a copy in the secure letters folder.  
5. If declining, respond promptly and, when possible, suggest alternative referees.

Policies & style  
Follow institutional FERPA/confidentiality rules, use letterhead, maintain factual tone with specific examples, and avoid sharing the letter with the applicant unless policy allows.
summaries.Humor Project:
Humor Project – Category Summary (120-180 words)

What belongs here  
• All messages tied to the ongoing “Humor Project”: joke or sketch pitches, script drafts, punch-up notes, comic storyboards, performance schedules, collaboration proposals, and budget or rights inquiries.  
• Senders are usually writers, comedians, editors, producers, agents, or partner brands exploring comedic content.

Typical intent & requests  
• Submit new material for consideration or revision.  
• Request creative feedback, approvals, or green-lights.  
• Coordinate meetings, table reads, rehearsals, or recording sessions.  
• Negotiate contracts, usage rights, or royalty terms.  
• Share press, sponsorship, or cross-promotion opportunities.

Handling & response strategy  
1. Acknowledge within 24 hrs; use a friendly, upbeat tone that still matches corporate professionalism.  
2. Triage:  
   • Creative matters → Lead writer/creative director.  
   • Scheduling/logistics → Project coordinator.  
   • Legal/rights → Legal or Business Affairs.  
3. Track all versions in the shared content repository; reference document ID/date in replies.  
4. Flag urgent production-impacting items (“needs approval by…”) as High Priority.  
5. Maintain confidentiality of unreleased material; watermark attachments when forwarding.

Key policies & constraints  
• Follow brand humor guidelines (no discriminatory or NSFW content).  
• Obtain written clearance before using third-party jokes or talent.
summaries.Adobe Project:
Adobe Project  
This folder holds all traffic related to our Adobe-based creative projects and licensing. Typical senders include internal designers, project managers, marketing leads, IT licensing, and occasionally Adobe account reps. Messages usually:  
• share or revise creative briefs, mock-ups, or asset links (PSD, AI, INDD, etc.)  
• request design time estimates, status updates, or approvals  
• flag technical or licensing issues (Creative Cloud access, plug-in compatibility)  
• confirm contract terms, renewal dates, or purchase orders for Adobe seats.  

Triage & response  
1. Scan subject line for due dates or “URGENT/REVIEW” tags; answer time-sensitive feedback within 4 business hours.  
2. File assets to the shared Creative Cloud library; avoid large attachments in replies.  
3. Log task or issue in Asana with owner and deadline, then acknowledge sender.  
4. Route license or billing questions to Procurement; CC IT for access problems.  

Policies & preferences  
Follow brand guidelines and version-control naming (Project_Version##_Initials). Maintain NDA confidentiality; never forward proprietary files outside the organization. Replies should be brief, action-oriented, and include the next milestone date.
summaries.CBL: I don’t have any sample messages to analyze, so I’m not yet sure what “CBL” stands for or what kinds of emails belong there. Could you share a few representative messages (just subjects and brief snippets are fine)? With that context I can write an accurate, concise summary covering themes, intent, handling strategy, and any policies that apply.
summaries.DoubleAgents:
DoubleAgents

What belongs here  
Messages involving individuals or firms that operate or represent two sides of a relationship (e.g., consultants working for competing clients, partner reps with dual allegiances, confidential informants). Threads often reference “dual mandate,” “conflict of interest,” or “intel for both teams.”

Typical sender intent  
• Share sensitive information while protecting sources  
• Negotiate terms that satisfy both parties without revealing all details  
• Request NDAs, code-names, or secure channels  
• Disclose or seek guidance on potential conflicts

Triage & response strategy  
1. Flag as HIGH-CONFIDENTIAL; restrict visibility to need-to-know recipients.  
2. Verify sender identity and authority before engaging.  
3. Acknowledge within 4 business hours; promise a fuller reply once compliance review is complete.  
4. Loop in Legal/Compliance for conflict-of-interest assessment and NDA drafting.  
5. Use encrypted channels for attachments or strategy details; never forward externally.

Key policies & style preferences  
• No PII or internal strategy in open text.  
• Refer to sensitive parties with agreed code names.  
• Maintain neutral, factual tone; avoid revealing biases or proprietary data.  
• Escalate any suspected policy breach immediately.
summaries.Other:
Category Purpose  
“Other” is a catch-all folder for messages that do not cleanly match any existing business, support, marketing, HR, finance, or personal categories. I file here one-off or low-volume topics, mixed-content threads, unexpected external pitches, newsletters from unknown senders, stray calendar invites, automated system notices, or any email whose intent is unclear after a quick scan.

Typical Sender Intent  
• General inquiries that don’t reference a known project  
• Cold outreach or partnership feelers  
• Miscellaneous notifications (e.g., platform updates, community digests)  
• Potential spam or phishing attempts

Triage & Response Strategy  
1. Skim for legitimacy: check sender domain, tone, and links.  
2. If actionable and relevant, re-route to the correct specialist folder or create a new tag if volume warrants.  
3. If useful but not urgent, flag “Read Later.”  
4. If irrelevant or suspicious, delete or mark as spam/phishing.  
5. When reply is warranted, acknowledge receipt, clarify intent, and redirect the sender to the appropriate contact or form.

Policy Notes  
Avoid sharing internal details until identity and purpose are verified. Maintain a neutral, concise reply style.
summaries.DAPL: I don’t have any example emails to analyze for the “DAPL” category. Could you forward or paste a few representative messages (subject line, sender, short excerpt)? With even 3-5 samples I can identify themes, intents, and the best triage/response approach.
summaries.speaking:
Speaking – Summary Guide (for all speaking-engagement requests)

What belongs here  
• Invitations to keynote, moderate, or sit on panels at conferences, webinars, podcasts, internal company meetings, universities, or media outlets.  
• Follow-ups about talk titles, abstracts, bios, headshots, travel or honorarium details.  
• Inbound pitches from event organizers, bureaus, or PR reps seeking our participation.

Sender intent & common asks  
• Secure my availability and interest.  
• Obtain a customized topic outline, fee quote, or signed speaker agreement.  
• Coordinate scheduling, travel, AV requirements, and promotional assets.  
• Request short-notice substitutions or pre-event prep calls.

Handling & triage  
1. Acknowledge within 24 h; thank them and request missing essentials (date, location, audience size, budget).  
2. Check calendar and strategic fit (target audience, visibility, revenue).  
3. Flag high-value or time-sensitive offers to leadership immediately; decline misaligned or unpaid gigs politely.  
4. Once accepted, move thread to “Confirmed Speaking” subfolder and loop in logistics coordinator.  
5. Use templated contracts and standard rate card unless exceptions approved.

Policies & style  
• Maintain courteous, concise tone; mirror sender’s formality.  
• No travel commitments without signed agreement.  
• Avoid definitive yes until all key terms are documented.
summaries.Karthik Sreedhar:
Karthik Sreedhar – Category Summary (120–180 words)

What belongs here  
All direct mail threads from Karthik Sreedhar (ks4190@columbia.edu), typically concerning research progress, code/results troubleshooting, draft reviews, or meeting logistics.

Sender intent & common requests  
• Report recent work, flag errors, or confirm that an issue is resolved.  
• Propose or adjust meeting times, often same-day.  
• Ask for quick feedback on experiments, analyses, or manuscript sections.  
• Share revised files or Git links that need review.

Triage & response strategy  
1. Reply within 24 h (same day if scheduling is time-sensitive).  
2. If the request is simple (e.g., “Can we meet at 3 pm?”) confirm in one line and add to calendar.  
3. For technical questions, acknowledge receipt, state when detailed feedback will come, then add action items to the project tracker.  
4. Keep the original subject line intact to preserve threading; prepend short tags like “[Reviewed]” when a task is complete.

Style & policy notes  
• Maintain a supportive, collegial tone; reinforce progress.  
• Use numbered lists for feedback and clear next-steps.  
• No PII or unpublished data should be forwarded outside the team without explicit consent.
summaries.Gui, George:
Gui, George

Scope: All correspondence sent by or specifically concerning George Gui—project updates, stakeholder questions, approvals, scheduling, or FYI threads where he is the primary author or focus.

Typical intent & requests: George usually informs the team of status changes, seeks quick clarifications, submits documents for review, or requests decisions/approvals on timelines, budgets, and technical approaches. Messages may also include meeting invites, follow-up notes, or escalations requiring prompt acknowledgement.

Triage & handling: Flag as high-priority and route to the relevant project lead or decision-maker within 4 business hours. For simple clarifications, reply directly with concise answers and copy any impacted teammates. For approvals or larger decisions, aggregate supporting docs into the existing project folder, draft a brief summary of implications, and forward to the exec sponsor. Always confirm receipt and next steps to George.

Style & policies: Use a courteous, action-oriented tone; provide bullet-point responses when multiple items are raised. Reference project codes instead of client names for confidentiality. Archive threads once action items are logged in the project tracker.
summaries.Lydia Chilton:
Category: Lydia Chilton

This label stores all messages sent by Prof. Lydia Chilton (or automated systems on her behalf). Typical content includes project guidance, feedback on drafts, meeting invitations, class-related announcements, deadline reminders, and requests for status updates or deliverables. Most emails are informational or action-oriented: they either convey decisions, provide resources, or ask for quick confirmation, documents, or scheduling.

Handling strategy  
• Read immediately and acknowledge within 12 hours; use a crisp, professional tone.  
• Extract concrete tasks (e.g., “revise section 2,” “add slides,” “schedule demo”) and add them to the project tracker.  
• If a meeting is requested, propose at least two time slots and attach a calendar invite once agreed.  
• For feedback on drafts, confirm receipt, then reply with an updated version or timeline for revisions.  
• If the email includes class announcements, forward or post them to the appropriate student channel unchanged.

Key practices  
• Preserve email threads for context; avoid deleting quoted text.  
• Follow any style or formatting instructions provided by Prof. Chilton exactly.  
• Escalate urgent blockers or resource constraints immediately.
summaries.get ken hired:
“Get Ken Hired” Folder – Summary (140 words)

Scope & Themes  
I file here any message related to supporting Ken’s job search: recruiter outreach, role referrals, networking introductions, interview invitations, requests for Ken’s résumé/portfolio, follow-ups on applications, and internal notes about openings that might suit him. Messages may come from HR staff, hiring managers, alumni, or contacts offering advice or leads.

Typical Intent & Requests  
Senders usually want one of three things:  
• Ken’s latest résumé, LinkedIn, or availability for a call/interview  
• Additional information (salary expectations, work eligibility, references)  
• Confirmation that a referral or introduction has been made

Handling & Response Strategy  
1. Acknowledge within one business day.  
2. Confirm receipt and supply the requested materials or schedule.  
3. Log the opportunity in the job-search tracker and CC Ken (and the referrer, if appropriate).  
4. Set a reminder to follow up one week before any stated deadline.

Policies & Style  
Keep responses concise, positive, and tailored to the role. Do not share Ken’s personal data beyond résumé and public profiles without explicit consent.
summaries.NYWIFT Membership:
NYWIFT Membership

Emails in this folder revolve around New York Women in Film & Television (NYWIFT) member matters. Typical messages include:

• New membership applications or status checks  
• Renewal reminders, payment confirmations, and lapse notices  
• Inquiries about member benefits, events, mentorships, or committee participation  
• Requests to update contact details, profile information, or membership tier  
• Announcements of networking mixers, panels, or volunteer opportunities targeted to members

Sender intent is generally to join, stay current, maximize benefits, or publicize member-exclusive opportunities.

Triage & response:
1. Confirm membership status in NYWIFT CRM before replying.  
2. For payments or renewals, reference the latest invoice, provide secure payment link, and log in Finance Tracker.  
3. Route benefit questions to Membership Services if unresolved within two emails or 48 hrs.  
4. Flag urgent lapsed-member issues for same-day follow-up to minimize churn.  
5. File event promos after adding dates to the shared calendar; reply only if RSVP or action required.

Style: Warm, supportive tone celebrating women in media; use inclusive language; close with “Thank you for supporting NYWIFT’s mission.”
summaries.George William Miller III:
George William Miller III: Category Summary (120–180 words)

Scope & Themes  
All correspondence sent by, forwarded from, or primarily concerning George William Miller III—whether personal, professional, or administrative. Typical content includes project updates, scheduling requests, approvals, document reviews, or status checks on joint initiatives. Messages referencing “George William Miller III” in the To, From, CC, BCC, or opening lines also belong here.

Sender Intent & Common Requests  
• Requesting decisions or sign-offs on deliverables  
• Proposing or confirming meeting times  
• Sharing background materials or performance metrics  
• Asking for clarification, resources, or next-step ownership  
• Notifying of travel, availability, or changes in scope

Handling & Response Strategy  
1. Triage by urgency: deadlines, blockers, or time-sensitive approvals receive same-day response; informational updates can be filed and acknowledged within 48 hours.  
2. Tag subfolders (e.g., “Action Needed,” “Awaiting Reply,” “Reference”) to keep his chain organized.  
3. Confirm receipt and outline next actions succinctly; use bullet points and anticipated timelines.  
4. Loop in relevant stakeholders only as needed to reduce noise.

Policies & Style  
Maintain a professional, collegial tone; mirror his formality level. Cite previous discussions or document versions for continuity. Refrain from sharing confidential information outside approved recipients and always encrypt attachments containing sensitive data.
summaries.Susan E McGregor:
This folder is a sender-specific bucket for everything that arrives from Susan E. McGregor, regardless of topic. Messages typically include:  • project or research updates, requests for feedback, or scheduling;  • questions about ongoing collaborations, resource needs, or decision approvals;  • shared documents, drafts, or reference links.  

Sender intent is usually to keep work moving—seeking quick clarifications, confirming next steps, or delivering materials for review.

Handling/triage:  
1. Scan for time-sensitive asks (deadlines, meeting polls) and flag them “High Priority.”  
2. File reference materials or drafts into the relevant project folder while leaving a copy in this category for audit.  
3. Reply within one business day; if deeper input is required, acknowledge receipt and give an ETA.  
4. Loop in additional stakeholders only as needed to avoid thread bloat.  

Style: concise, solution-oriented replies; use bullet points for action items and clearly label next steps and owners.
summaries.Xiaofeng Wang:
This category is reserved for all correspondence from Xiaofeng Wang—likely a single high-priority stakeholder such as a collaborator, supervisor, or key client. Expect emails that: 
• share project updates, data or document drafts  
• request feedback, approvals, or technical input  
• propose or confirm meetings, deadlines, or next steps.  

Sender intent is usually to move joint work forward, obtain timely decisions, or clarify details.  

Handling strategy  
1. Set inbox rule to auto-file messages here while marking them “Important.”  
2. Aim to read within four business hours and reply within one business day.  
3. If an action is required, create a task with due date matching any stated deadline; otherwise acknowledge receipt and outline next steps.  
4. Escalate or loop in additional team members only when their input is explicitly requested or essential to unblock progress.  

Style preferences  
• Keep replies concise: bullet points for decisions, numbered comments on documents.  
• Use the original subject line for continuity; prepend “RE:” or “UPDATE:” only when status has materially changed.
summaries.Gary L. Zamchick:
This folder captures any message where Gary L. Zamchick is the sender or primary correspondent. Expect project-specific updates, feedback on in-progress work, scheduling requests, document shares, or strategic guidance—often requiring timely acknowledgement to keep collaboration moving. Messages may include attachments (drafts, slide decks, contracts) or links that need quick review.

Handling/triage: 
1. Scan on receipt for action items or deadlines; flag “urgent” if a same-day response or file turnaround is implied.  
2. File supplemental materials into the relevant project repository and confirm download success.  
3. Reply within 24 hours (or sooner when time-sensitive), summarising next steps, proposed timelines, and any clarifying questions.  
4. If the topic crosses teams, loop in stakeholders early, copying Gary to maintain transparency.

Style: Keep responses concise, solution-oriented, and appreciative of his input; use bullet points for clarity and avoid jargon. No confidential data should be forwarded without explicit consent.
summaries.Frank Rose:
Frank Rose is a high-priority individual contact folder. Messages here are direct exchanges with Frank—typically project status updates, requests for information or assets, feedback on deliverables, and meeting or travel scheduling. Senders are almost always Frank himself, occasionally his assistant, and their intent is to obtain prompt clarification, approvals, or progress confirmation.  

Handling strategy:  
• Scan for deadlines or explicit questions first; flag anything with requested turnaround times shorter than 48 hours.  
• Reply within one business day, even if only to acknowledge receipt and give an ETA.  
• Keep responses concise, action-oriented, and reference prior threads or attached documents to maintain context.  
• If an ask requires team input, forward immediately with clear ownership and copy Frank on the delegated action.  

No special legal or branding constraints are specified, but maintain a courteous, collaborative tone and preserve the existing subject line when possible to aid thread continuity.
summaries.Eugene Wu:
This label is for all one-to-one correspondence with Eugene Wu—e.g., project guidance, strategic feedback, resource or data requests, approvals, or time-sensitive scheduling. Messages typically seek quick clarification, status updates, decisions, or deliverables. I should treat them as high-priority and respond within 4 business hours.

Triage workflow:  
1. Scan for explicit asks (answers, documents, meeting times, sign-offs).  
2. If an action or deliverable is requested, create/ update the relevant task ticket and note due dates.  
3. If cross-functional input is needed, loop in stakeholders immediately, summarizing context in the thread.  
4. File pure FYI notes under “Reference” subfolder; archive after logging key takeaways.

Response style: concise, bullet-friendly, with clear next steps and committed timelines. Confirm receipt when additional time is required, then follow up by the promised date. Escalate blockers promptly.

No confidential data should be forwarded outside the team without Eugene’s written consent.
summaries.Garud Iyengar, Data Science Institute:
Category: Garud Iyengar, Data Science Institute

Emails in this folder typically come from Prof. Garud Iyengar, Data Science Institute (DSI) leadership, affiliated faculty, or institute staff. Themes include research collaborations, grant or proposal reviews, curriculum or committee updates, seminar/event planning, student advising, data-science program logistics, and strategic initiatives involving DSI. Senders usually seek: (1) feedback or approval on attached drafts, budgets, or policy documents; (2) confirmation of participation in meetings, talks, or panels; (3) quick data or metrics for reports; or (4) coordination on student or postdoc matters.

Triage/response: 
• Flag high-priority notes from Prof. Iyengar or DSI directors for same-day acknowledgment.  
• Add meeting invites or deadlines to the shared calendar immediately.  
• Route specialized questions (IRB, finance, IT) to the appropriate university office while keeping the sender cc’d.  
• For collaborative drafts, suggest edits in track-changes and return within 48 hours.

Key policies: uphold university confidentiality, avoid sharing unpublished research, and use professional, concise language; include clear subject tags (e.g., “REVIEW NEEDED – Proposal XYZ”) to streamline follow-ups.
summaries.Esther Schwartz: No example emails were provided for the “Esther Schwartz” category, so I don’t yet have enough information to describe its themes, intents, or recommended handling approach. Please supply a few representative messages (or at minimum subject lines and brief excerpts) so I can generate an accurate, actionable summary.
summaries.Jesse Green:
Jesse Green (folder)  
This category captures all correspondence sent by or concerning Jesse Green—a key stakeholder whose messages usually drive active projects or decision-making. Expect project updates, clarifications, document reviews, meeting invites, and occasional approvals or escalations. The sender’s intent is typically to exchange time-sensitive information, request status or deliverables, or provide strategic direction.

Triage & handling  
• Flag new mail as “High” and read same day; many threads are blockers for ongoing work.  
• If an action or answer is required, acknowledge within 4 business hours, confirm next steps, and log tasks in the project tracker.  
• Route technical questions to the relevant SME immediately, CC’ing Jesse to maintain visibility.  
• Archive threads once all action items are closed, but retain attachments in the shared project folder.

Response style  
Keep replies concise yet comprehensive, using bullet points for clarity and clear deadlines for follow-ups. Address Jesse by name, maintain a professional tone, and avoid jargon unless previously agreed upon. Uphold confidentiality—do not forward without permission and redact sensitive client data when necessary.
summaries.Mark Habana:
This folder captures all correspondence originating from or addressed to Mark Habana. Messages typically include status updates on joint projects, quick questions, meeting invitations, or requests for approvals/resources. The sender’s intent is usually to coordinate work, share progress, or obtain a timely decision.

Handling guidelines  
• Scan new mail from Mark as soon as it arrives; he is likely a key stakeholder or collaborator.  
• If the email contains an explicit request (e.g., feedback, sign-off, scheduling), acknowledge within 2–4 business hours and add the task to your tracker.  
• For informational updates, log any action items and archive the thread once noted.  
• Escalate immediately if the message references blockers, budget impacts, or client-facing issues.  

Response style  
Keep replies concise, numbered where multiple points are addressed, and confirm next steps or deadlines. Copy only necessary teammates to reduce noise. Follow all internal confidentiality and data-handling policies when forwarding or quoting his messages.
summaries.Grant Proposals:
Grant Proposals

Emails in this folder are from researchers, nonprofits, or internal teams seeking funding, submitting full proposals or letters of intent, requesting feedback on draft narratives/budgets, or asking about eligibility, deadlines, and required forms. Typical attachments include project descriptions, budgets, biosketches, and compliance certifications.

Sender intent: secure financial support, receive confirmation of receipt, clarify guidelines, or negotiate terms. Common asks: extension requests, allowable cost questions, required signatures, and reporting schedules.

Handling: 
1. Acknowledge receipt within one business day, quoting the submission ID and deadline.  
2. Save attachments to the Grants drive and log key data (PI, amount, due date) in the tracker.  
3. If incomplete, flag “Action Needed” and request missing items.  
4. Route complete packages to the review committee lead, copying Finance and Compliance.  
5. Update status weekly and notify applicants of decisions only after formal approval.

Policies: maintain confidentiality, avoid promising funding prematurely, use the organization’s proposal template for feedback, and keep all correspondence in the grant CRM. Replies should be concise, neutral, and reference relevant guideline sections.
summaries.Daniel E. Osgood:
This folder captures all correspondence sent directly by Daniel E. Osgood—a high-priority individual stakeholder (e.g., project sponsor, senior collaborator, or VIP client). Messages typically include: 
• Progress check-ins, status updates, or feedback on ongoing projects  
• Requests for data, documents, or clarifications  
• Scheduling or rescheduling of meetings, calls, or reviews  
• Approvals, sign-offs, and strategic guidance  

Sender intent is usually time-sensitive and directive, expecting prompt acknowledgment and clear next steps.

Handling guidelines  
1. Auto-flag Daniel’s emails as “High Priority” and surface them at the top of my inbox.  
2. Acknowledge receipt within 2 business hours, even if a full answer requires more research.  
3. If action items involve others, immediately forward or assign tasks with a concise summary and deadline.  
4. Log any decisions or approvals in the project tracker for audit purposes.  

Response style: professional, concise, and solution-oriented; reference specific project identifiers when relevant and confirm deadlines or deliverables to avoid ambiguity.
summaries.Rithisorn, Kathleen:
Category: “Rithisorn, Kathleen”

This folder captures one-to-one correspondence with Kathleen Rithisorn—likely a key stakeholder, partner, or colleague on active projects. Messages typically revolve around status updates, document reviews, scheduling, or decision approvals rather than broad team announcements. Senders (often Kathleen herself) usually want quick clarifications, confirmation of next steps, or delivery of specific files.

Triage/handling:
• Scan for action items, dates, or questions on first open; flag anything requiring a reply or deliverable within 24 hours.  
• If the email contains meeting logistics, copy details to the calendar immediately.  
• For document feedback, save the latest version in the shared drive and reply with a brief acknowledgment plus expected turnaround time.  
• Close simple yes/no or confirmation requests the same day to keep momentum.

Response style:
Maintain a concise, professional tone; use bullet points for multi-step answers, reference attached files explicitly, and include clear “Next step” statements. Escalate only if decisions affect budget, scope, or compliance policies.
summaries.Reichherzer, Carolin Maria - reicm004:
This folder is reserved for all correspondence sent by or directly concerning Carolin Maria Reichherzer (user ID reicm004). Messages typically include project-status updates, technical questions, document reviews, scheduling requests, and approvals that require my input or acknowledgment. Senders—usually Carolin herself or colleagues copying her—aim to secure timely feedback, confirm decisions, or share deliverables.

Triage guidelines  
• Scan new mail for due dates, action verbs (“review,” “approve,” “confirm”) and attachments.  
• Flag anything with a hard deadline or executive visibility as “High Priority”; respond or delegate within one business day.  
• Archive informational messages after noting key points in the relevant project tracker.

Response strategy  
• Acknowledge receipt promptly, specify next steps, and give a realistic turnaround time.  
• Use concise subject prefixes (e.g., “APPROVED – …” or “FOLLOW-UP REQUEST – …”) to signal status.  
• Keep replies in the original thread to preserve context; attach annotated documents when returning edits.

Compliance/Style  
Follow internal confidentiality guidelines and version-control rules for shared files. Use the established corporate tone: professional, courteous, and action-oriented.
summaries.CHI PC:
CHI PC  

This folder houses all correspondence related to my role on the ACM CHI Program Committee. Typical messages come from Technical Program Chairs, subcommittee chairs, area chairs, or the PCS system and cover: review and meta-review assignments, bidding/conflict checks, deadline reminders, policy updates (confidentiality, ethics, COI), shepherding instructions, and logistics for PC or meta-reviewer meetings. Sender intent is usually to secure timely reviews, clarify guidelines, resolve conflicts, schedule discussions, or gather votes on paper decisions.

Triage:  
1. Flag anything with imminent deadlines (review due, rebuttal window, meeting RSVP) and add to task list.  
2. File informational broadcasts (policy memos, templates) for later reference.  
3. Escalate policy/conflict questions directly to the relevant chair.  

Response strategy: reply within 24 h, acknowledge assignment, confirm availability, provide concise updates, and keep all discussion on-record within the committee thread.  

Key policies: maintain strict confidentiality of submissions and reviewer identities; disclose and recuse from any COI immediately; follow CHI tone guidelines—professional, constructive, and bias-aware.
summaries.John Hewitt:
John Hewitt category collects all messages sent directly by, cc’ing, or chiefly concerning John Hewitt—typically a senior stakeholder and recurring collaborator. Content usually includes project-status inquiries, feedback on deliverables, meeting or travel scheduling, approval requests, and occasional quick FYI notes or resources he forwards. His intent is generally to obtain timely updates, confirm decisions, or delegate next steps.  

Handling strategy  
• Auto-flag as “High” priority; strive to acknowledge within 4 business hours, provide full answers inside 24.  
• Immediately extract action items, add to task tracker, and set follow-up reminders.  
• If clarification is needed, batch questions into one concise reply to minimize thread volume.  
• For schedule changes, update shared calendars before replying.  

Style & policies  
• Use a succinct, results-oriented tone, bullet points for options, and attach any supporting docs.  
• Copy relevant team members only; avoid broad lists unless decisions affect them.
summaries.Atlassian:
Atlassian-category mail covers all communication generated by Atlassian cloud or server products (Jira, Confluence, Bitbucket, Trello, Opsgenie, Statuspage) as well as Atlassian’s sales, billing, and trust teams. Typical messages include: • product-update bulletins, release notes, maintenance and outage advisories • account notices—trial expirations, license renewals, invoices, payment failures • security alerts (vulnerability disclosures, suspicious-login warnings, required password or token resets) • automated project activity (issue assignments, pull-request reviews, sprint reminders) • support-ticket traffic and SLA escalations.

Sender intent is primarily informational or action-oriented: they want us to review changes, pay or update billing details, apply patches, or respond to support threads.

Triage guideline:
1. Flag security or outage notices as P1 and route to DevOps/security within 1 hour.
2. Forward billing or contract items to Finance; acknowledge receipt same day.
3. Route active support tickets to the assigned engineer and update Jira comments.
4. Archive low-urgency product news after scanning for roadmap impact.

Respond briefly, reference ticket/issue keys, avoid sharing internal credentials, and mirror Atlassian’s thread style (inline replies, bullet summaries).
summaries.Smaranda Muresan:
Smaranda Muresan  
This folder is for any conversation either sent by, addressed to, or primarily concerning Prof. Smaranda Muresan (research collaborator / adviser). Typical messages include:  • Research coordination—draft papers, data‐set questions, experiment results, revision deadlines, and authorship confirmations.  • Teaching logistics—guest-lecture invitations, syllabus updates, TA assignments, or student reference requests.  • Administrative items—grant paperwork, budget justifications, IRB renewals, or travel arrangements for joint conferences.  The sender’s intent is usually to exchange scholarly feedback, set or confirm timelines, or obtain approvals/signatures.  

Triage & response:  
1. Skim for hard deadlines (conference submissions, funding agency dates) and flag “High Priority.”  
2. If technical feedback is requested, acknowledge within 24 hrs, provide estimated turnaround, then respond with inline comments or tracked-change files.  
3. For scheduling, propose 2-3 slots in recipient’s time zone and send calendar invite once agreed.  
4. Route administrative forms to department staff once countersigned; archive the final PDF.  

Style: keep replies concise, numbered when multiple points, and include the latest document link in cloud storage rather than large attachments.
summaries.Eugene Wu via Vimcal:
This folder captures all scheduling-related messages sent through Vimcal on behalf of Eugene Wu. Expect automated or semi-automated emails that: 1) propose meeting times, 2) confirm a slot you selected, 3) send calendar invites or updates, or 4) notify you of reschedules or cancellations. The sender’s intent is purely logistical—pinning down or adjusting a meeting with Eugene or his team—so messages are concise and action-oriented.

Handling guide  
• First, scan the proposed options; if a time works, use the embedded “Select” or “Confirm” button, or reply with alternate availability within 24 hours.  
• Once confirmed, verify that the event appears correctly on your calendar (time zone, location/URL, attendees).  
• If you need to reschedule, click the provided link rather than replying manually; Vimcal will automatically circulate new options.  
• File completed threads under “Scheduled” and leave any pending choices in this category for quick follow-up.

No confidential data is typically included, but follow standard company calendar-sharing policies when forwarding or CC’ing others.
summaries.lance.weiler@columbia.edu:
Category: lance.weiler@columbia.edu

Scope  
Emails originating from Lance Weiler at Columbia University. Typical threads involve creative research initiatives, course or lab logistics, cross-disciplinary collaborations, event planning, and requests for feedback on documents, prototypes, or student work.

Sender intent & common asks  
• Schedule or confirm meetings, workshops, guest lectures, or studio sessions.  
• Share project updates, draft syllabi, or grant proposals and request comments or approvals.  
• Solicit resources—e.g., references, introductions, budget estimates, or tech support.  
• Coordinate deadlines, deliverables, or speaking engagements.

Triage & response strategy  
1. Acknowledge within one business day, even if only to confirm receipt and give an ETA.  
2. Capture action items in task manager; flag time-sensitive items (events, funding deadlines).  
3. When feedback is requested, note turnaround expectations and reply with tracked-change docs or bullet-point comments.  
4. For scheduling, propose specific slots in recipient’s time zone; include Zoom/room details once set.

Key guidelines  
• Maintain collegial, solutions-oriented tone; keep replies under 200 words unless analysis is required.  
• Use Columbia’s shared drive links instead of large attachments.  
• CC only essential collaborators to limit thread noise.
summaries.Michael Roger:
Category purpose  
All messages sent by or on behalf of Michael Roger—regardless of topic—are filed here to keep one-to-one communication with this key stakeholder in a single place.

Typical content & sender intent  
• Project status checks, deadline updates, or resource requests  
• Clarification questions on current tasks or deliverables  
• Approvals or sign-off on documents, budgets, or schedules  
• Strategic guidance, meeting invitations, or feedback on prior work

Triage & response guidelines  
1. Open immediately; treat as high-priority unless clearly informational.  
2. Acknowledge receipt within 2 business hours, even if a full answer will follow later.  
3. If action is required, enter a task in the project tracker and note the due date referenced.  
4. Loop in additional team members only when their input is essential; otherwise keep the thread direct and concise.  
5. When sending replies, use a clear subject prefix (“Re: <topic> – Status/Decision Needed”) and provide numbered responses for multi-part questions.

Style & policy notes  
Adopt a professional yet succinct tone, include data or attachments Michael requests, and avoid forwarding sensitive details outside approved recipients.
summaries.LinkedIn:
LinkedIn

Emails in this folder are system-generated notifications and one-to-one messages routed through LinkedIn. They include: connection requests, InMails, recruiter outreach, partnership or sales pitches, job or candidate alerts, group or event invitations, mentions/comments on posts, skill endorsements, anniversaries, and weekly digests.

Sender intent ranges from networking or recruiting to promoting products, scheduling informational calls, or driving attendance to webinars and industry events. Common requests: “Let’s connect,” “Can we discuss a role/opportunity?,” “Interested in a quick call/demo?,” or “Join/attend/confirm.”

Triage guidelines  
1. Connection & InMail from known contacts, prospects, or recruiters: respond within 1 business day; personalize acceptance or thanks.  
2. Qualified business or partnership pitches: forward to BD/Sales queue with brief context.  
3. Recruiter/job leads of interest: flag for HR, reply with availability or résumé.  
4. Routine platform notifications (likes, endorsements, anniversaries, newsletters): mark read or auto-archive.  

Maintain a courteous, concise tone; avoid sharing confidential company details.
summaries.Fong, Hortense:
Fong, Hortense  
Scope: All messages sent by or on behalf of Hortense Fong. Expect one-to-one correspondence rather than broadcast announcements.

Typical content & intent  
• Project-specific questions, status checks, document reviews, or scheduling requests  
• Approvals, feedback, or change requests on deliverables you own  
• Occasional FYI forwards or introductions to third parties

Handling & response strategy  
1. Prioritize: Treat as high-importance; aim to acknowledge within 4 business hours.  
2. Clarify: If action items are unclear, summarize your understanding in the reply and request confirmation to avoid misalignment.  
3. Track: Log commitments and deadlines in the project tracker immediately after replying.  
4. Escalate: Loop in relevant team leads when scope, budget, or timeline changes are discussed.  
5. Archive: Once closed, file the thread in the project folder and mark the category “complete” to keep the inbox tidy.

Style & policies  
• Use a courteous, concise tone; mirror her formatting style (bullet lists or numbered points) for readability.  
• Include subject prefixes like “REVIEW,” “DECISION NEEDED,” or “UPDATE” to signal urgency.
summaries.Ash Cleary:
Ash Cleary

This category captures all correspondence sent by or on behalf of Ash Cleary—a primary stakeholder whose messages typically concern project direction, deliverable reviews, scheduling, or decision approvals. Emails here usually contain clear action items such as requesting status updates, supplying feedback, providing new requirements, or confirming logistics (meetings, travel, invoices). The sender’s intent is to obtain timely information or authorization that keeps joint work moving forward.

Handling/Triage
• Flag as “High Priority” and acknowledge within 4 business hours; aim to close the loop inside one business day.  
• Identify explicit asks (e.g., documents needed, dates to confirm) and either fulfill directly or delegate with tracked follow-up.  
• If the message affects scope, budget, or timeline, brief the project lead and update the shared tracker.

Response Strategy
Reply in a concise, decision-oriented style: confirm receipt, answer each point in bullets, state next steps with owner and deadline. Escalate only if legal or budget approval is required.
summaries.Xu, Xuhai \\Orson\\:
This folder stores every thread exchanged with Dr. Xuhai “Orson” Xu. Messages typically cover joint research tasks, manuscript or grant drafts, dataset/code hand-offs, meeting logistics, or quick technical questions. Senders—usually Orson himself or assistants copying him—seek status updates, feedback on attached documents, availability for calls, or approval to proceed.

Triage approach  
• Scan immediately for deadlines, submission windows, or meeting proposals and add them to my calendar or task list.  
• If a file or draft is attached, acknowledge receipt within 12 hours and note when I expect to return edits.  
• For simple yes/no or scheduling queries, reply the same business day to keep projects moving.  
• Flag any confidential data or unpublished results to ensure files stay inside approved repositories.

Response style  
Use a collegial, concise tone; reference specific section/page numbers when commenting on drafts; leave a clear action item at the end. Follow institutional data-sharing and citation policies, and CC relevant co-authors only when necessary.
summaries.Joann Shi:
Joann Shi: Category Overview (120–180 words)

Scope  
All mail threads sent by or directly concerning Joann Shi—typically from her company address or calendar aliases. Content usually revolves around ongoing projects, scheduling logistics, document reviews, or strategic feedback.

Typical Sender Intent  
• Share project updates or status reports  
• Request information, decisions, or approvals  
• Propose meeting times or agenda changes  
• Provide feedback on deliverables or attach revised documents

Handling/Triage Strategy  
1. Scan for time-sensitive asks (deadlines, meeting polls) and flag for same- or next-business-day response.  
2. Add actionable items to the task tracker; link thread for context.  
3. Reply inline, acknowledging receipt and outlining next steps; cc relevant stakeholders only.  
4. Archive or label once actioned to maintain a clean inbox.  

Response Style  
Use a concise, collaborative tone—bullet points for clarity, clear owners and due dates. Reference any attached version numbers. Confirm receipt of critical files.

Key Policies  
Follow internal confidentiality rules; do not forward externally without permission. Retain threads for audit compliance (minimum 12 months).
summaries.Shar Simpson:
Shar Simpson is an executive-level stakeholder, so this category captures any message sent by Shar directly or where she is the primary topic (e.g., “FYI from Shar,” “Per Shar’s request”). Content usually revolves around strategic decisions, project status, resourcing, approvals, or time-sensitive feedback. Expect concise directives, questions requiring context, or attachments (presentations, financials, draft statements).  

Sender intent: convey high-level guidance, request quick clarification, delegate tasks, or confirm deliverables. Occasionally she may forward external threads that need your awareness or action.  

Handling/triage:  
• Flag as priority—aim to read and triage within two business hours.  
• Create follow-up tasks in the tracker; assign owners and due dates.  
• If clarification is needed, reply the same day with targeted questions. Otherwise acknowledge, outline next steps, and commit to a deadline.  
• Loop in relevant team leads only; avoid broad “Reply All.”  

Style preferences: keep replies under 200 words, use bullet points for status, include clear subject tags (e.g., “UPDATE,” “DECISION NEEDED”), and attach concise summaries rather than raw files when possible.
summaries.Alexis M Newman (Google Docs):
This folder holds all Google Docs–related notices that mention or originate from Alexis M. Newman—e.g., share invitations, “comment added,” “request for access,” or “assigned task” alerts. The sender’s intent is usually to (1) grant me access to a document Alexis owns, (2) flag a section for my review, or (3) solicit edits, approval, or sign-off. Occasionally I’ll see automated access-request emails when third parties try to open one of Alexis’s files.

Triage quickly:  
• New share invites or access requests → open the doc, verify relevance, accept or decline within 4 business hours.  
• Comment or suggestion threads → skim the email preview, click “Open in Docs,” and reply or resolve in-document; acknowledge via email only if broader context is needed.  
• Task assignments → add to my project tracker and mark the Gmail notice “Done.”

Follow our collaboration rules: keep edits in Suggesting mode, avoid replying with sensitive data, and cc Alexis only when a wider project decision or scheduling impact arises.
summaries.Claire Shipman and Katrina Armstrong:
Category summary – “Claire Shipman and Katrina Armstrong” (executive-level contacts)

What fits: Any message sent by, addressed to, or prominently concerning Claire Shipman or Katrina Armstrong—including scheduling requests, media inquiries, thought-leadership collaborations, speaking invitations, research/clinical updates, or follow-ups on ongoing joint projects.

Sender intent & common asks:  
• Arrange meetings, interviews, or panel appearances  
• Share drafts, briefing decks, or data for review/approval  
• Request quotes, endorsements, or strategic guidance  
• Coordinate logistics for conferences, book/press activity, or philanthropic initiatives

Handling & response strategy:  
1. Treat as VIP—acknowledge within 2 business hours.  
2. Confirm receipt, note any deadlines, and flag to the relevant executive lead or chief-of-staff immediately.  
3. Maintain a single, clean thread per topic; include concise status bullets when forwarding.  
4. Use calendar holds (tentative) while awaiting principal confirmation.  
5. File sensitive attachments in the secure drive and reference link only.

Key policies: Preserve confidentiality (no 3rd-party forwarding without consent); follow brand voice—clear, courteous, and results-oriented; abide by media-embargo or IRB rules when research data are shared.
summaries.Hongseok Namkoong:
This folder holds any correspondence sent by, forwarded from, or chiefly concerning Prof. Hongseok Namkoong. Typical messages include research collaboration proposals, manuscript or grant‐writing feedback, requests for data or code, meeting-scheduling threads, recommendation-letter inquiries, and administrative notices (e.g., seminar logistics, travel reimbursements). The sender’s intent is usually to obtain a timely decision, technical input, or confirmation of availability.

Triage:  
1. Scan for hard deadlines (submission dates, travel bookings, letter due dates) and flag accordingly.  
2. If technical questions or draft reviews are requested, acknowledge within 24 hrs and provide an estimated turnaround.  
3. For scheduling, propose 2-3 time slots or direct the sender to the shared calendar link.

Responses should be concise, collegial, and reference file names or line numbers when discussing documents. Follow institutional confidentiality and data‐sharing policies; do not circulate unpublished results beyond approved collaborators.
summaries.Alexis M Newman:
Alexis M Newman is treated as an “individual-sender” folder rather than a topic folder. Anything where Alexis is the sender—or the primary recipient but clearly directing a reply to you—belongs here. Messages typically revolve around project status, deliverables, scheduling, or quick decisions that need your acknowledgement or sign-off. The usual intent is to inform, request clarification, or obtain approval; time-sensitive asks (e.g., “can you review by EOD?”) are common.

Triage: 
1. Scan for explicit deadlines or action verbs (“approve,” “review,” “confirm”) and flag with a due-date tag.  
2. If the email contains attachments or links to shared docs, save them to the relevant project folder immediately.  
3. Reply within one business day; if a longer review is required, send a brief receipt (“Got it—will revert by <date>”).  
4. Loop in other stakeholders only when Alexis requests it or when policy requires cross-team visibility.

Style: keep responses concise, answer each bullet or question inline, and use Alexis’s preferred greeting “Hi Alexis,” unless the thread already dictates a different tone.
summaries.Christopher Xi:
Christopher Xi: This folder is reserved for all one-to-one or group threads in which Christopher Xi is either the sender or the primary correspondent. Typical content includes project status updates, clarifying questions, document reviews, meeting invitations, and decision or approval requests. Senders (usually Christopher) aim to share progress, obtain quick feedback, schedule touch-points, or flag blockers requiring my input.

Triage/handling: 
1. Scan immediately for time-sensitive asks (deadlines, meeting polls, approval checkpoints) and add to task list or calendar within the day.  
2. Reply within 24 hours with concise, bullet-pointed answers or next steps; loop in additional stakeholders only when necessary.  
3. File completed threads or reference materials in the folder; mark open items with a follow-up flag.  

Style preferences: maintain a professional yet collegial tone, reference action items clearly (“Next step: … by DD/MM”), and keep subject lines intact for continuity. No personal data should be forwarded outside the team without consent.
summaries.Baishakhi Ray:
Baishakhi Ray – Category Summary (120–180 words)

What belongs here  
All messages that originate from or are directly addressed to Prof. Baishakhi Ray, or that explicitly discuss her research projects, lab logistics, or collaborative efforts. Typical subjects include paper drafts, reviewer comments, grant‐proposal coordination, meeting invitations, reference requests, and student mentoring updates.

Sender intent & common requests  
Prof. Ray usually writes to (1) share or request feedback on technical documents, (2) schedule or confirm meetings, (3) delegate action items related to ongoing research, or (4) seek quick status updates on deadlines. Expect concise, task-oriented language and a preference for rapid turnaround on critical items (≤48 hrs).

Handling & triage  
Mark as High Priority. Immediately note any hard deadlines, add tasks to the project tracker, and acknowledge receipt within one business day—even if only to give an estimated response time. Flag items requiring multi-party input and loop in relevant collaborators early. Archive completed threads to the project’s shared folder for future reference.

Policies & style preferences  
Reply with clear bullet-point action items, include the latest document version link, and keep technical discussions in-thread (avoid new chains). Maintain confidentiality on unpublished results and grant details.
